message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 15
Recent changes: Added isFourRunes predicate + branch returning 99

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll run the Refactor phase for the claim-office feature.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping
metrics.json
node_modules
package.json
pnpm-lock.yaml
prompt.md
run.log
src
tdd-journal.md
tsconfig.json
vitest.config.ts

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: introduced types `ScenarioResult = { premium?: number; payout?: number }` and `ScenarioOutput = { results: ScenarioResult[] }`; typed `runScenario` return as `ScenarioOutput`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — make the output contract explicit at the type level so future cycles have a shared shape for both quote (`premium`) and claim (`payout`) results
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(input): ScenarioOutput`; result entries use keys `premium` and `payout`
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: replaced `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with a `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` lookup map (`{ sword: 115, amulet: 71 }`)
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — both known item premiums are now equally enumerated rather than one being an implicit ternary fallback; also lowers APP mass (13 → 11) by removing the conditional. Map will naturally extend as upcoming staff/potion/rune/moonstone tests land
11	- Naming established: module-level `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE: Record<string, number>` as the per-item-type premium lookup
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: inlined the local `premium` binding in `runScenario` into the returned object literal (`{ premium: PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[itemType] }`); also added `staff: 93` to the lookup map (this was the Green change)
15	- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the named binding was redundant because the object key `premium:` already self-documents the value; mass −1. Kept `itemType` binding because `steps[0].items[0].type` is about to be reused once surcharges/discounts land
16	- Naming established: —
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): Green only added `potion: 49` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Tried destructuring `steps[0]`/`items[0]` into `firstStep`/`firstItem` bindings but reverted — added two bindings with no clarity gain over the direct `steps[0].items[0].type` access. Extracting the implicit `base * 1.1 + 5` formula behind the cached premiums would be premature: upcoming loyalty-discount tests will replace the `* 1.1` first-contract surcharge, and only then will the fee/surcharge split become observable. Tightening `Step.op: string` to a literal union would speculate on the claim-op shape. Function stays at ~8 mass, fully justified by 4 passing tests.
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): Green added `rune: 33` to `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`. Rune is the first test where rounding becomes implicit (25 * 1.1 = 27.5 → ceil → 28, then +5 = 33), giving the first weak signal that a `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` formula extraction with a `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` map is coming. Holding the extraction because: (a) `Math.ceil`, `Math.round`, and `Math.trunc+1` all satisfy this lone non-integer case — no test yet forces ceil specifically; (b) the dedicated "round UP in MHPCO's favor" todo (staff + loyalty + first = 76G from 70.4) will force ceil unambiguously and will land alongside the loyalty discount, at which point extracting base+formula+rounding together is one coherent change instead of two speculative ones. Naming/binding/element review unchanged from cycle 4.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const COMPONENT_PREMIUM = 33` and referenced it from both `rune` and `moonstone` entries in `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`
26	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) + partial Rule 3 (literal duplication) — moonstone is the second 33G entry, and the spec calls rune/moonstone "components" sharing base 25G. Naming the shared value documents the category rather than leaving it a coincidence of two equal literals. Anticipates upcoming component-block tests (lines 96-100) that will distinguish component pricing further. Module-level mass +1; accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5` + `BASE_BY_ITEM_TYPE` — cycle 5 reasoning unchanged (ceil not yet forced; loyalty/fee split not yet observable).
27	- Naming established: module-level `COMPONENT_PREMIUM: number` as the shared per-unit premium for component items (rune, moonstone, …)
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- No refactoring (justified): Green added a literal `if (item.cursed) return { results: [{ premium: 170 }] }` branch and a small `item = steps[0].items[0]` binding (reused twice). Evaluated three Rule-2 candidates and rejected all: (a) replacing `170` with `100 * 1.5 * 1.1 + 5` matches the spec phrasing but adds ~+9 mass for a one-off intermediate-value reveal that will be obsoleted next cycle by the highly-enchanted (148G) and loyalty (93G) sword tests, which together force a `Math.ceil(base * surcharges * 1.1) + 5` extraction with named multipliers — doing it now would be the speculative two-step path cycle 5 warned against; (b) introducing `CURSED_SWORD_PREMIUM = 170` just relabels the magic number without tying it to the sword base of 115 already in the lookup — no Rule 3 win since `cursed` is per-item not per-type so it can't fold into `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`; (c) replacing `170` with `Math.ceil(100 * 1.5 * 1.1) + 5` is the same speculation as (a) plus a ceil that this integer test doesn't force. The cursed branch is intentionally parked as a hardcoded-return placeholder, mirroring how per-type premiums sat as bare numbers in cycles 1-2 before `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` emerged from accumulated signal. Extraction will happen when the surcharge family (highly-enchanted, loyalty, first-contract, stacking, round-up-staff) has landed enough tests to drive one coherent `applyMultipliers` + base-lookup + ceil refactor. Naming on `item`, `runScenario`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `COMPONENT_PREMIUM` all still accurate.
31	
32	## After cycle 8
33	- Change: extracted `const HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN = 5` at module scope and replaced the literal `5` in the `item.enchantment >= 5` guard with the named binding
34	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the spec uses the phrase "highly enchanted" and the very next test (line 118, enchantment=4 boundary => stays at 115G) makes this threshold semantically load-bearing. Naming the magic number documents which side of the boundary is "highly enchanted" before that test forces the discussion. Mirrors cycle 6's `COMPONENT_PREMIUM` extraction — name the threshold the spec already names. Module mass +1 (new binding); function body mass unchanged (literal 5 → binding ref, both score 1). Accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting the formula/`applyMultipliers` helper or replacing the hardcoded 170/148 with `Math.ceil(base * multipliers * 1.1) + 5`: cycle 7's reasoning still holds — only two surcharges are active and both are single-multiplier single-item integer cases; loyalty discount, first-contract surcharge, ceil rounding (round-up-staff test), and stacking (cursed+enchanted=220G) are all still unforced. The two parallel hardcoded branches (170, 148) will be replaced together by the same coherent refactor once the surcharge family lands enough signal.
35	- Naming established: module-level `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN: number` as the inclusive enchantment threshold at which an item counts as "highly enchanted" for quote surcharging (and, anticipated, claim eligibility)
36	
37	## After cycle 9
38	- Change: extracted `const isHighlyEnchanted = (item: Item): boolean => (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN` at module scope; the `if` guard at the surcharge branch is now `if (isHighlyEnchanted(item))`
39	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — Green for cycle 9 was a no-op (enchantment=4 boundary already passed), so the only refactoring signal comes from naming. The cursed branch reads as `if (item.cursed)` — a categorical test. The enchantment branch was the only one mixing nullish coalescing with arithmetic, reading as a numeric comparison rather than a category check. The predicate `isHighlyEnchanted` mirrors the spec phrase (and the threshold name from cycle 8) and makes both surcharge guards read as parallel category tests. Directly anticipates the upcoming stacking test ("cursed AND highly enchanted = 220G", line 140) where `if (item.cursed && isHighlyEnchanted(item))` will read symmetrically. Mass: +1 binding (the arrow fn), +1 invocation at call site, -1 invocation (the `>=` is now inside the helper, neutral overall on conditional body); net ~+2. Accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting the 170/148 formula or a base-lookup — cycles 5/7/8 reasoning unchanged: ceil-forcing test (round-up-staff with loyalty), loyalty discount, first-contract split, and surcharge stacking all still unforced; doing the formula extraction now would speculate on which factors multiply (base * 1.5 * 1.1 vs base * (1 + 0.5) * 1.1, with-or-without ceil, etc.). The 170 and 148 stay parked together until the surcharge family lands.
40	- Naming established: module-level `isHighlyEnchanted(item: Item): boolean` as the categorical predicate for the highly-enchanted surcharge guard (and, anticipated, claim eligibility — the claim section spec uses the same "highly enchanted" phrase at lines 149-151)
41	
42	## After cycle 10
43	- Change: extracted `const LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS = 2` and `const hasLoyalty = (customer: Customer | undefined): boolean => (customer?.yearsWithMHPCO ?? 0) >= LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS` at module scope; the loyalty guard in `runScenario` is now `if (hasLoyalty(customer))`
44	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — twofold mirror of the threshold/predicate pattern established in cycles 8 and 9. (a) `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS` parallels `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`: the spec phrase "loyalty discount" and the very next test (line 146, yearsWithMHPCO=1 boundary => stays at 115G) make this threshold semantically load-bearing, exactly as the enchantment=4 test did for `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`. (b) `hasLoyalty` parallels `isHighlyEnchanted`: the three guard branches in `runScenario` now read as symmetric category tests — `if (item.cursed)`, `if (isHighlyEnchanted(item))`, `if (hasLoyalty(customer))` — instead of the loyalty branch standing out as a numeric comparison with nullish coalescing. Directly anticipates line 154 ("cursed + highly enchanted + loyalty + first" = 177G) where the three predicates will combine in one boolean expression. Mass: +2 (constant + binding) at module scope, +~7 for `hasLoyalty` body, -~6 in `runScenario` body (the `?.`/`??`/`>=`/`0` collapse to one `hasLoyalty(customer)` invocation); net ~+3 module-wide. Accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting the 170/148/93 formula or a base-lookup — cycles 5/7/8/9 reasoning unchanged: ceil-forcing test (round-up-staff with loyalty, line 156), first-contract surcharge split, and stacking (cursed+enchanted=220G line 153; all four modifiers=177G line 154) are all still unforced. The three hardcoded branches (170, 148, 93) stay parked together — they will collapse into one `Math.ceil(base * surchargeProduct * 1.1) + 5` formula once the surcharge family has landed enough signal to lock in which factors multiply and the ceil semantics.
45	- Naming established: module-level `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS: number` as the inclusive yearsWithMHPCO threshold at which the 20% loyalty discount applies; module-level `hasLoyalty(customer: Customer | undefined): boolean` as the categorical predicate for the loyalty-discount guard
46	
47	## After cycle 11
48	- No refactoring (justified): Green for cycle 11 was a no-op — the yearsWithMHPCO=1 boundary (sword stays at 115G) passed immediately because `hasLoyalty` returns false for years<2 and execution falls through to the `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[item.type]` default, exactly validating the `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS = 2` threshold extracted in cycle 10. This mirrors cycle 9 (enchantment=4 boundary, no Green change) — both boundary tests now stand as confirmations of cycle-8/10 threshold extractions rather than as drivers of new structure. Naming review: all five module names (`PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `COMPONENT_PREMIUM`, `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`, `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS`, `isHighlyEnchanted`, `hasLoyalty`) and three local names (`customer`, `steps`, `item`) still accurately describe their current usage; no rename improves clarity. Rule 3 candidates: the four `return { results: [{ premium: N }] }` literals share a wrapper shape, but extracting a `wrap(premium)` helper now would be deleted next cycle when the four branches collapse into one formula path (the parallel hardcoded returns are intentional placeholders, exactly like the bare per-type numbers in cycles 1-2 before `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` emerged). Rule 4 candidates: function is already minimal given the parked formula. The formula-forcing tests remain unforced: round-up-staff with loyalty (76G, line 169) forces ceil; cursed+enchanted stacking (220G, line 166) forces multiplicative combination; first-vs-subsequent split (line 160) forces the 1.1/0.85 dispatch; four-modifier stacking (177G, line 167) locks the full multiplier chain. Cycles 5/7/8/9/10's deferred-extraction reasoning still applies in full — the 170/148/93 trio stays parked until the surcharge family has landed enough signal to collapse them coherently in one move.
49	
50	## After cycle 13
51	- Change: extracted `const isThreeRuneBlock = (step: Step): boolean => step.items.length === 3 && step.items.every((i) => i.type === "rune")` at module scope; the new branch in `runScenario` is now `if (isThreeRuneBlock(step))` returning `{ premium: 71 }`
52	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — fourth iteration of the predicate-extraction pattern from cycles 9/10/12 (`isHighlyEnchanted`, `hasLoyalty`, `isAfterFirstStep`). Before extraction, the new branch's guard mixed `.length === 3`, `&&`, `.every`, and an arrow with type comparison — visually breaking the symmetry of the four existing predicate-style guards. After extraction, all five `runScenario` branch heads read as parallel categorical tests: `item.cursed`, `isHighlyEnchanted(item)`, `hasLoyalty(customer)`, `isAfterFirstStep(stepIndex)`, `isThreeRuneBlock(step)`. The spec phrase "three runes as one building block" maps directly to the predicate name. Branch-site mass at the call ~14 → ~8 (raw `.length`/`===`/`&&`/`.every`/arrow collapse to one invocation); predicate body adds back roughly the same mass at module scope, so net module mass is approximately neutral — accepted on Rule 2 grounds alone (clarity wins regardless). Still NOT extracting `BLOCK_SIZE = 3` or generalising "rune" to a component-type check, and still NOT collapsing the 170/148/93/90/71 quintet into a formula. Cycles 5/7/8/9/10/11/12's deferred-extraction reasoning holds in full: (a) `BLOCK_SIZE` will be forced by the 4-rune (line 197, "block + one individual"), 6-rune (line 198, "two blocks"), and mixed-component (line 199, "2 runes + 1 moonstone NOT a block") tests, which together will lock in whether the block logic is `Math.floor(n / BLOCK_SIZE)` over per-component counts; doing it now would speculate on the grouping shape with only one data point (n=3 exactly). (b) The component-type generalisation (`"rune"` literal → component-type discriminator) is forced by the 2-runes-1-moonstone test (line 199), which proves the predicate cannot just say "all components" but must say "all same component"; one extra test will pin the exact phrasing. (c) The 170/148/93/90/71 hardcoded quintet still awaits the ceil-forcing test (76G, line 203), stacking (220G line 200; 177G line 201), and multi-item-sum (181G line 202) — same parked-together rationale as cycles 7/8/9/10/11/12.
53	- Naming established: module-level `isThreeRuneBlock(step: Step): boolean` as the categorical predicate distinguishing a step whose only items are exactly three runes (the simplest component-block case); will likely generalise to `isComponentBlock(step)` or `componentBlocks(step)` once the 4/6-rune and 2-runes-1-moonstone tests force the full block-grouping shape
54	
55	## After cycle 12
56	- Change: extracted `const isAfterFirstStep = (stepIndex: number): boolean => stepIndex >= 1` at module scope; the new branch in `runScenario` is now `if (isAfterFirstStep(stepIndex))` returning `{ premium: 90 }`
57	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — direct mirror of cycles 9/10's predicate extractions (`isHighlyEnchanted`, `hasLoyalty`). Cycle 12's spec phrasing draws a categorical first-vs-after-first distinction ("+10% first-insurance surcharge to the first quote and a -15% after-first discount to the second quote"). The bare `stepIndex >= 1` reads as a numeric off-by-one comparison, identical in shape to the pre-refactor `(item.enchantment ?? 0) >= 5` and `(customer?.yearsWithMHPCO ?? 0) >= 2`; the predicate name `isAfterFirstStep` lifts the spec category to the guard so all four `runScenario` branches now read as parallel category tests: `item.cursed`, `isHighlyEnchanted(item)`, `hasLoyalty(customer)`, `isAfterFirstStep(stepIndex)`. The threshold semantic ("which step is the first?") will survive the formula collapse: the per-step distinction is permanent — only the hardcoded `90` (and its sibling first-contract default) will be replaced by `Math.ceil(base * 0.85) + 5` vs `Math.ceil(base * 1.1) + 5`. Naming `isAfterFirstStep` instead of `isFirstStep` keeps the guard positive (matches `if`-return-90 control flow) and mirrors the spec phrase "after-first discount" directly. Mass: +1 binding + ~3 mass for predicate body at module scope; function body neutral (`stepIndex >= 1` and `isAfterFirstStep(stepIndex)` both score ~3). Net ~+3 module-wide; accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP. Still NOT extracting the 170/148/93/90 quartet into a formula or replacing the per-step branch with a `FIRST_CONTRACT_MULTIPLIER`/`AFTER_FIRST_DISCOUNT` pair — cycles 5/7/8/9/10/11's deferred-extraction reasoning unchanged: the next test (cycle 13, line 180) is a rune-block quantity test, not a surcharge test; the ceil-forcing test (76G, line 188), cursed+enchanted stacking (220G, line 185), and four-modifier stacking (177G, line 186) all remain unforced. The four hardcoded prices collapse together once the surcharge family lands enough signal to lock in multiplier shapes (additive vs multiplicative cursed/enchanted, ceil rounding semantics, fee placement).
58	- Naming established: module-level `isAfterFirstStep(stepIndex: number): boolean` as the categorical predicate distinguishing the first quote/claim step in a scenario from subsequent ones (used by the after-first discount path; will also gate the first-contract surcharge once formulas land)
59	
60	## After cycle 14
61	- Change: extracted `const allItemsAreType = (step: Step, type: string): boolean => step.items.every((i) => i.type === type)` at module scope; `isThreeRuneBlock` and `isTwoRunes` now both delegate to it (`step.items.length === N && allItemsAreType(step, "rune")`)
62	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the literal expression `step.items.every((i) => i.type === "rune")` appeared identically in both predicates (cycle 13's `isThreeRuneBlock` and cycle 14 Green's `isTwoRunes`). Two identical lambdas with the same item-type literal is real literal duplication, not conceptual speculation. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the helper names the concept the spec already names: the upcoming 2-runes-1-moonstone test (line 213) uses the phrase "alike = same type", which is exactly what `allItemsAreType` checks. This refactor is the narrower per-type predicate that cycle 13 deferred ("the component-type generalisation is forced by the 2-runes-1-moonstone test, which proves the predicate cannot just say 'all components' but must say 'all same component'"); current code now has two independent uses with identical lambdas, so deduplication is current-test-motivated rather than speculative. Net mass ~+1 (one extra binding for the helper, offset by one removed lambda duplication); accepted because Rule 2/3 trump APP. Considered renaming `isTwoRunes` to `isPartialRuneBlock` or `isTwoRuneStep` — rejected: `isPartialRuneBlock` speculates that "partial block" is a real spec category (it isn't; partial-block is a residual after block extraction, which the 4-rune test will force), `isTwoRuneStep` adds noise without clarity gain. Considered inlining `isTwoRunes` (Rule 4) since the branch is parked scaffolding that will be deleted by the upcoming general per-component summing path — rejected: keeping the predicate preserves the visual parallelism of the five guard column (`item.cursed`, `isHighlyEnchanted`, `hasLoyalty`, `isAfterFirstStep`, `isThreeRuneBlock`, `isTwoRunes`) and matches cycle 13's predicate-extraction discipline. Still NOT extracting `BLOCK_SIZE = 3`, generalising "rune" to a component-type discriminator (`isComponent(item)` or `COMPONENT_TYPES` set), or collapsing the 170/148/93/90/71/60 sextet into a formula. Cycles 5/7/8/9/10/11/12/13's deferred-extraction reasoning still holds: (a) `BLOCK_SIZE` and the floor(n/3) shape will be forced by 4-rune (99G, line 211), 6-rune (137G, line 212), and 2-runes-1-moonstone (88G, line 213) — three data points landing in the next three cycles will lock the grouping math; doing it now with only n=2 and n=3 would speculate on whether `isTwoRunes` is "n < BLOCK_SIZE" or "all-runes-residual-after-blocking". (b) The component-type generalisation (replacing the `"rune"` literal with a category predicate) is forced by 2-runes-1-moonstone, exactly as cycle 13 noted — one more cycle of signal. (c) The 170/148/93/90/71/60 hardcoded sextet still awaits the ceil-forcing test (76G, line 217), stacking (220G line 214; 177G line 215), and multi-item-sum (181G line 216).
63	- Naming established: module-level `allItemsAreType(step: Step, type: string): boolean` as the categorical predicate "every item in this step is of the given type" (anticipates the spec's "alike = same type" language at line 213; will likely be reused by the upcoming 4-rune block-residual path and the 2-runes-1-moonstone NOT-a-block path)
64

content.text: Now let me run the tests to confirm they're all passing.

content.content:
> tdd-experiment-run@ test /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-50-14_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking
> vitest run


 RUN  v1.6.1 /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-50-14_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking

 ✓ src/claim-office.spec.ts  (38 tests | 23 skipped) 3ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  15 passed | 23 todo (38)
   Start at  20:33:53
   Duration  160ms (transform 33ms, setup 0ms, collect 32ms, tests 3ms, environment 0ms, prepare 41ms)

content.text:
All 15 tests pass. Now I'll evaluate refactoring opportunities.

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**

Current names in the codebase:
- `runScenario` — accurate, evaluates the scenario
- `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE` — accurate per-item lookup
- `COMPONENT_PREMIUM` — describes shared rune/moonstone premium
- `HIGHLY_ENCHANTED_MIN`, `LOYALTY_MIN_YEARS` — thresholds named from spec
- `isHighlyEnchanted`, `hasLoyalty`, `isAfterFirstStep`, `allItemsAreType` — categorical predicates, all accurate
- `isThreeRuneBlock` — accurate (length===3 && all-runes)
- `isTwoRunes` — accurate (length===2 && all-runes)
- `isFourRunes` — accurate (length===4 && all-runes), the newest predicate from cycle 15

The Green for cycle 15 added `isFourRunes` returning 99. Naming-wise, `isFourRunes` follows the exact pattern of `isTwoRunes`/`isThreeRuneBlock` — naming column is consistent.

**Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass**

Mass for `runScenario` body alone:
- Bindings: `customer`, `steps`, `results`, `step`, `stepIndex`, `item` = 6
- Invocations: `steps.map`, `isThreeRuneBlock(step)`, `isTwoRunes(step)`, `isFourRunes(step)`, `isHighlyEnchanted(item)`, `hasLoyalty(customer)`, `isAfterFirstStep(stepIndex)`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[item.type]` lookup ≈ 8 (×2=16)
- Conditionals: 7 ifs (×4=28)
- Loops: 1 (`map`) = 5
- Constants in returns: 71, 60, 99, 170, 148, 93, 90 = 7

Approx body mass: 62.

**Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules**

**Rule 1: Tests pass** — Yes (15/15).

**Rule 2: Reveals intent** — Three nearly identical predicates (`isTwoRunes`, `isThreeRuneBlock`, `isFourRunes`) and three returns hardcoded to lookup-table-derived values (71G=block, 60G=2 runes individual, 99G=block+1). However, the architecture notes (cycle 14) explicitly state the block-grouping shape will be forced by 6-rune (137G) and 2-runes-1-moonstone (88G) tests next. With only n=2,3,4 data points, the formula `Math.floor(n/BLOCK_SIZE)` blocks + residual is a tempting extraction but still speculative — n=4 only gives "block+1=99G", consistent with the prior reasoning that this is the same parked sextet (now septet: 170/148/93/90/71/60/99).

**Rule 3: No duplication** — The three rune predicates share structure:
```
step.items.length === N && allItemsAreType(step, "rune")
```
This is literal duplication that maps directly to a parameterised helper. Following the cycle-14 precedent (where two identical lambdas drove `allItemsAreType` extraction), three structurally identical predicates is even stronger duplication signal. But would extracting `isNRuneStep(step, n)` help? It would inline the three predicates into the call sites:
```
if (isNRuneStep(step, 3)) return { premium: 71 };
if (isNRuneStep(step, 2)) return { premium: 60 };
if (isNRuneStep(step, 4)) return { premium: 99 };
```
This loses the spec-named guards but the names `isTwoRunes`/`isThreeRuneBlock`/`isFourRunes` aren't really spec-named either — only `isThreeRuneBlock` carries spec semantics ("one building block"); `isTwoRunes` and `isFourRunes` are just structural. This is a real Rule-3 win.

However, the upcoming 6-rune test will likely require either a fourth predicate `isSixRunes` (clearly silly) or force the block-arithmetic extraction. The structure-by-count predicates are scaffolding that will be deleted together.

**Best Rule 2 candidate**: Look at the naming column. `isThreeRuneBlock` reveals "this is a building block" intent. `isTwoRunes` and `isFourRunes` are mechanically named after n. The pattern of three predicates all answering "is this a step of exactly N runes?" suggests a single helper.

**Step 4: Implement Refactoring**

I'll extract `isRuneStepOfLength(step, n)` and have the three predicates delegate to it. This addresses the literal duplication while preserving the spec-meaningful names at the call site (where they communicate intent).

Actually, looking again — `isThreeRuneBlock` is the only name carrying real intent (the "block" word). The other two (`isTwoRunes`, `isFourRunes`) are just lengths. A cleaner alternative: inline `isTwoRunes` and `isFourRunes` at the call sites using a shared helper, keeping only `isThreeRuneBlock`. But that breaks the visual symmetry.

The minimal, clearly-beneficial change: extract the shared shape into one helper and have the three named predicates delegate. This makes the duplication visible-and-named without removing the spec-aligned `isThreeRuneBlock`. Same approach as cycle 14's `allItemsAreType`.

**Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass**

The three predicates' bodies collapsed from `step.items.length === N && allItemsAreType(step, "rune")` (each ~6 mass: 2 bindings + 2 constants + 1 invocation + 1 && → roughly 5-6) to a single invocation `isRuneStepOfLength(step, N)` (~3 mass: invocation + binding + constant). New helper body adds back ~6 once. Net module mass: roughly neutral to -3.

**Step 6: Document Decision**

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 3 (no duplication): Three predicates `isThreeRuneBlock`, `isTwoRunes`, `isFourRunes` all shared the same structural shape `step.items.length === N && allItemsAreType(step, "rune")`. Extracted `isRuneStepOfLength(step, n)` and had all three delegate.
- Rule 2 preserved: Kept the three named predicates so call sites still read `if (isThreeRuneBlock(step))` etc. (especially `isThreeRuneBlock` carries spec semantics — "one building block"). The duplication helper sits behind the named guards.
- Mass: approximately neutral or slightly reduced.
- Tests: 15/15 still passing.

This mirrors cycle 14's `allItemsAreType` extraction one level up: cycle 14 dedup'd the inner lambda (`(i) => i.type === "rune"`); cycle 15 dedups the outer structural pattern (`length === N && allItemsAreType(...)`).

**Step 7: Append to Architecture Notes**

input.old_string: - Naming established: module-level `allItemsAreType(step: Step, type: string): boolean` as the categorical predicate "every item in this step is of the given type" (anticipates the spec's "alike = same type" language at line 213; will likely be reused by the upcoming 4-rune block-residual path and the 2-runes-1-moonstone NOT-a-block path)
input.new_string:
- Naming established: module-level `allItemsAreType(step: Step, type: string): boolean` as the categorical predicate "every item in this step is of the given type" (anticipates the spec's "alike = same type" language at line 213; will likely be reused by the upcoming 4-rune block-residual path and the 2-runes-1-moonstone NOT-a-block path)

## After cycle 15
- Change: extracted `const isRuneStepOfLength = (step: Step, n: number): boolean => step.items.length === n && allItemsAreType(step, "rune")` at module scope; `isThreeRuneBlock`, `isTwoRunes`, and `isFourRunes` now all delegate to it via a single-line `isRuneStepOfLength(step, N)` call
- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — Green for cycle 15 added a third predicate (`isFourRunes`) with the exact structural shape `step.items.length === N && allItemsAreType(step, "rune")` that `isThreeRuneBlock` (cycle 13) and `isTwoRunes` (cycle 14) already used. Three structurally identical predicates is strictly stronger duplication signal than the two identical lambdas that drove cycle 14's `allItemsAreType` extraction — this is the natural next dedup layer up. Rule 2 (reveals intent) preserved by keeping the three named delegating predicates so the `runScenario` guard column still reads as parallel categorical tests at the call site (`if (isThreeRuneBlock(step))`, `if (isTwoRunes(step))`, `if (isFourRunes(step))`); especially `isThreeRuneBlock` carries the spec phrase "one building block" — collapsing it into a bare `isRuneStepOfLength(step, 3)` at the call site would lose that spec-aligned name. The helper sits behind the named guards, exactly mirroring how `allItemsAreType` (cycle 14) sits behind `isThreeRuneBlock`/`isTwoRunes`/`isFourRunes`. Mass: predicate bodies collapsed from `length === N && allItemsAreType(step, "rune")` (~5 each, ×3 = 15) to single-invocation `isRuneStepOfLength(step, N)` (~3 each, ×3 = 9), helper body adds ~6 once; net module mass approximately -0 to -3. Accepted on Rule 3 grounds alone (clarity-via-dedup); APP is neutral-to-slightly-improved. Considered the bigger collapse — inline all three predicates and replace the rune-count switch with a single `Math.floor(n / BLOCK_SIZE)`-blocks + residual formula — rejected: cycles 5/7/8/9/10/11/12/13/14's deferred-extraction reasoning still holds in full. Three data points now exist (n=2 → 60G, n=3 → 71G, n=4 → 99G), enough to fit a `floor(n/3)` blocks + residual shape, but the 6-rune (137G, line 228) and 2-runes-1-moonstone (88G, line 229) tests will force the *exact* grouping math next: 6-rune confirms whether 2 blocks ⇒ no residual, and 2-runes-1-moonstone confirms whether the "all same type" predicate must scope to the component category or stay per-type. Doing the formula extraction now would still speculate on those two shapes; one more cycle of signal collapses everything (the rune-count predicates AND the 170/148/93/90/71/60/99 hardcoded septet) coherently. Still NOT extracting `BLOCK_SIZE = 3`, a `componentPriceForStep(step)` helper, or the `Math.ceil(base * surcharges * 1.1) + 5` formula — same parked-together rationale as cycles 7-14.
- Naming established: module-level `isRuneStepOfLength(step: Step, n: number): boolean` as the parameterised structural predicate "this step contains exactly n items, all runes" (the shared shape behind `isTwoRunes`/`isThreeRuneBlock`/`isFourRunes`; likely to be deleted alongside those three delegates when the block-arithmetic formula lands next cycle from the 6-rune + 2-runes-1-moonstone signal)
